title: Renewing the Alliance: A Healthier Trans-Atlantic Relationship
content:
Metaphors for the troubled trans-Atlantic relationship abound, describing it as a marriage, a divorce, or a parent and child navigating an empty nest. All these comparisons highlight a shared reality: the dynamic between the United States and Europe is morphing into something entirely new. It has become increasingly clear that the world is not returning to the post-Cold War status quo. However, too much of the debate continues to focus on limiting this transition and fretting over worst-case scenarios. Policymakers on both sides seem reluctant to explore what a healthy, reconfigured relationship should actually look like.

Instead of dreading change, leaders must smooth out the erratic seesaw of partisan approaches to European diplomacy. The constant flip-flopping between U.S. administrations creates bad feelings and the impression that America is an unreliable partner. Rather than promising a return to past dominance, politicians should imagine a robust, friendly relationship based on equality. Different administrations must also decide whether they want a cultural war with Europe or a reliable defense partner. Choosing conflict will only breed acrimony that ultimately undermines critical international security efforts.

Moving forward, policymakers must recognize shared interests rather than assuming U.S. influence in Europe is limitless. Both sides benefit from resisting Chinese global hegemony, sustaining European stability, and ensuring mutual economic growth. Treating European nations as equal partners, rather than dependencies, perfectly describes a highly functional trans-Atlantic relationship. There is no inherent contradiction between America's commitment to NATO and the idea that Europe should primarily defend itself. As Dwight D. Eisenhower noted in 1951, Western Europe cannot rely exclusively on American units to guard its far frontiers forever.
